raw_case:
Imaging of persistent bilateral occipital sinus with absent bilateral transverse sinus
Persistent occipital sinus with absent/ hypoplastic bilateral transverse sinus is an extremely rare finding in adults; less than 10 cases have been reported. We present a case of a 28-year-old male who was brought to the emergency department in a post-ictal state associated with blurring of vision and 1 vomiting episode. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with angiography and venography of the brain was advised, and the brain parenchyma was found to be anatomically normal. Incidental findings of the persistent occipital sinus with absent/ hypoplastic bilateral transverse sinus were made using venography imaging of the brain. This is a type of fetal presentation of venous blood flow. It is paramount to neurosurgeons because a prominent occipital sinus with an absent bilateral transverse sinus might change the surgical approach for the posterior fossa lesions.

Case report

A 28-year-old male patient, a shopkeeper by profession, was brought to the emergency department by his mother with complaints of teeth clenching for approximately 15 minutes, associated with 1 episode of vomiting and blurring of vision. The patient recalls no memory of the episode.The patient was confused but oriented to time, place, and person. The respiratory rate was 16 breaths per minute and was normal. Blood pressure was 124/82 mm of mercury, and heart rate was 76 beats per minute.The patient was a chronic alcoholic and had a history of drinking a minimum of 90ml of whisky daily for 12 years. His last drink was 1 day before the hospital visit. He has no significant medical history and is not a known case of systemic illness. The family history reveals nothing significant. There was no history of fever, head trauma, any known illness, or lack of sleep. There was no history of any medication or drug intake. He was lean-built, and there were no known risk factors for cerebral venous thrombosis other than the chief complaints provided.A complete blood count, liver function test, kidney function test, and vitamin B12 test were performed, all within normal limits as shown in Table 1.A provisional diagnosis of seizure was made, and a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) and magnetic resonance venogram (MRV) of the brain was advised to rule out any organic cause.MRI and MRA of the brain revealed the normal appearance of the brain parenchyma and its arterial supply. MRV revealed the absence of bilateral transverse sinus with persistent occipital sinus dividing into 2 and draining into the marginal sinus and the internal jugular veins. A sigmoid sinus was present bilaterally and drained into the internal jugular veins. A condylar vein was present, which was seen at the origin of the sigmoid sinus bilaterally, as shown in Fig. 1, Fig. 2, Fig. 3.A neurologist's call was made the next day when the patient was oriented to time, place, and person, and any organic cause for the above complaints was ruled out. The patient was given a detailed explanation of his low-risk category for the development of similar symptoms and was advised to take sodium valproate to reduce the chances of recurrence further.The patient refused any treatment because of monetary issues and was discharged with advice to follow up after 2 months or if a similar episode recurs.

Discussion

Venous blood from the brain reaches systemic circulation by venous channels. Numerous smaller channels drain into the bigger venous sinuses, connected to the systemic circulation via internal jugular veins. Transverse sinus, occipital sinus, superior sagittal sinus, and straight sinus form the torcular herophili a/k/a confluence of sinuses [1].The occipital sinus is prominent in the developing fetus and empties into the internal jugular veins through the marginal sinus. At the time of birth, the occipital sinus regresses, and the transverse sinus drains into the internal jugular veins through the sigmoid sinus, which forms the main venous drainage pathway. However, in very few people, the fetal presentation of the venous drainage system, i.e., occipital sinus draining into the marginal sinus and terminating into the internal jugular veins, persists [2,3].We present a case of incidental finding of persistence of bilateral occipital sinus and regression of bilateral transverse sinus in a patient presenting to us in a post-ictal state.This case report demonstrates the MRV appearance of the prominent bilateral occipital sinus draining into the bilateral marginal sinus and terminating into the bilateral jugular veins with a condylar vein at the origin of the sigmoid sinus bilaterally. This appearance is extremely rare; less than 10 such cases with imaging features have been reported in the literature.Dural venous sinuses are a bunch of vascular channels or sinuses draining the circulating venous blood from the cranial cavity. They return the blood from the head into the systemic circulation. Meningeal and periosteal layers of the dura matter contain 7 major venous sinuses: cavernous sinus, superior sagittal sinus, straight sinus, inferior sagittal sinus, sigmoid sinus, transverse sinus, and superior petrosal sinus [[4], [5], [6]].Embryologically, the occipital sinus is a minor venous channel most prominent in the fifth month of intrauterine life. They originate from the medial portions of the transverse sinus and the primitive torcula. With the advancement of the gestational age, occipital sinuses progressively reduce in caliber and number, and only a few individuals are born with the prominent occipital sinus. Normally, the occipital sinus lies in the midline, but it is not unusual to witness an occipital sinus not in the midline [2,3].The occipital sinus tracks inferiorly from the torcula to the marginal veins and then empties into the jugular, suboccipital, or paravertebral venous plexus [2]. The occipital sinus receives some blood from the spine and spinal cord by communicating with the posterior internal vertebral plexus and is considered an emissary vein. The occipital sinus works as a substitute venous drainage pathway when the venous return via the internal jugular vein is affected. The occipital sinus becomes the dominant pathway for venous drainage in cases of absent transverse sinus [1].There is no gender predilection associated with the prominence of the occipital sinus, and patients are mostly asymptomatic. Noncontrast computed tomography (NCCT) plays no role in the diagnosis. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) might show a clear anatomical depiction and help identify the anomaly. MRV is the investigation of choice displaying complete anatomy and other associated venous anomalies [7].Knowledge about the persistence of the occipital sinuses with absent transverse sinuses is crucial in various approaches for patients undergoing surgical interventions for the posterior fossa lesions, including the retro-sigmoid approach [8].

Patient consent

An informed verbal and written consent was obtained from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 28-year-old male, shopkeeper.
- **Chief Complaint:** Post-ictal state with vomiting and blurring of vision.
- **History of Present Illness:** A single episode of teeth clenching lasting 15 minutes, followed by vomiting and blurring of vision. No memory of the event.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** Chronic alcohol consumption (90 mL whisky daily for 12 years). Last alcohol intake one day prior to the visit.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Confused but oriented to time, place, and person. Vital signs were normal.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Blood investigations (complete blood count, liver function, kidney function, vitamin B12): Normal.
  - MRI and MRA: Normal brain parenchyma and arterial supply.
  - MRV: Persistent occipital sinus with absent/hypoplastic bilateral transverse sinus. The occipital sinus divided and drained into the marginal sinuses and internal jugular veins. Sigmoid sinus and condylar veins were present bilaterally.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- The initial diagnosis was a **seizure** based on the post-ictal presentation and episode of teeth clenching. Seizures can be provoked by various triggers, including alcohol withdrawal.
- **Cerebral venous thrombosis (CVT):** Considered but excluded due to normal lab results, absence of known risk factors, and no evidence of thrombosis or infarction on imaging.
- **Structural brain abnormalities:** Ruled out through normal MRI appearance.
- **Alcohol-related seizure:** Likely as the patient has a history of chronic alcohol consumption and his last drink was one day prior, potentially indicating withdrawal.
- **Incidental finding of persistent occipital sinus with absent transverse sinus:** Identified on MRV. This vascular anomaly is usually asymptomatic and unrelated to the presenting symptoms.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Incidental finding of a persistent occipital sinus with absent bilateral transverse sinus. The symptom of seizures was likely attributable to alcohol withdrawal.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Incidental finding of persistent occipital sinus with absent bilateral transverse sinus; alcohol-related seizure likely.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Summarize Key Findings:** A 28-year-old male shopkeeper presented with a post-ictal state following a 15-minute episode of teeth clenching.  He experienced vomiting and blurred vision afterward.  He has no memory of the event.  Significant personal history of chronic alcohol consumption (90mL whisky daily for 12 years), with last intake one day prior. Physical exam shows confusion but orientation.  Blood tests, MRI, and MRA are normal.  MRV shows persistent occipital sinus and absent/hypoplastic bilateral transverse sinus.

<step 2> **Analyze Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The chief complaint points towards a seizure-like event. Teeth clenching is suggestive of tonic-clonic activity.  The post-ictal state with vomiting, blurred vision, and confusion further supports this. Amnesia for the event is also typical of seizures. The duration of 15 minutes for the teeth clenching episode might be considered prolonged for a typical generalized tonic-clonic seizure, but could be a subjective estimation or include the entire seizure and immediate post-ictal phase.

<step 3> **Evaluate Past Medical, Personal, and Family History:**  No past medical or family history of neurological disorders is reported, which reduces the likelihood of idiopathic epilepsy or inherited conditions as primary causes. However, the significant personal history of chronic alcohol consumption is highly relevant. Chronic alcohol use can lead to alcohol withdrawal seizures, even if the last intake was just one day prior.  While 24 hours post-last drink might be slightly longer than the peak withdrawal seizure window, individual variations exist, and chronic heavy alcohol use can predispose to seizures even with a relatively short period of abstinence.

<step 4> **Analyze Physical Examination Findings:**  Confusion in the post-ictal phase is expected.  Orientation to time, place, and person suggests that the confusion is likely resolving and not due to a persistent underlying neurological or psychiatric condition. Normal vital signs are reassuring and rule out acute systemic issues in the immediate post-ictal period.

<step 5> **Interpret Ancillary Test Results:**
    - **Normal Blood Investigations:**  Normal complete blood count, liver function, kidney function, and vitamin B12 rule out common metabolic disturbances (electrolyte imbalance, uremia, hepatic encephalopathy), infection, and B12 deficiency as the primary cause of the seizure.
    - **Normal MRI and MRA:**  Normal brain parenchyma and arterial supply on MRI and MRA effectively rule out structural brain lesions (tumors, stroke, malformations) and major vascular abnormalities as the cause of the seizure. This makes primary epilepsy or structural lesion-induced seizures less likely.
    - **MRV Findings (Persistent Occipital Sinus, Absent/Hypoplastic Transverse Sinus):** These MRV findings are anatomical variations of the cerebral venous sinuses. While atypical venous drainage patterns can sometimes be associated with increased intracranial pressure or venous congestion in certain conditions, in isolation and with normal MRI/MRA, they are less likely to be the direct cause of a single, provoked seizure.  It is important to consider if these findings are incidental or potentially contributory.  Given the absence of other signs of venous hypertension (like papilledema, which is not reported and likely would have been noted in a post-ictal confused patient), and normal MRI parenchyma, these venous variations are more likely to be incidental anatomical variants, rather than the primary cause of the seizure. However, altered venous drainage could theoretically lower seizure threshold in some individuals, particularly in the context of other risk factors.

<step 6> **Formulate Differential Diagnoses:** Based on the information, the differential diagnoses include:
    - **Alcohol Withdrawal Seizure:** Highly probable given the history of chronic alcohol consumption and the timing relative to last alcohol intake.
    - **Idiopathic Epilepsy:** Less likely given the onset at age 28 and no family history, but still possible.  However, the single episode makes this less likely as a first presentation of unprovoked epilepsy.
    - **Sleep Deprivation Seizure:** Shopkeepers may have irregular sleep patterns, and sleep deprivation can lower seizure threshold. However, this is less specific than alcohol withdrawal in this context.
    - **Metabolic Seizure:**  Largely ruled out by normal blood tests.
    - **Structural Lesion-Induced Seizure:**  Largely ruled out by normal MRI and MRA.
    - **Venous Sinus Thrombosis or related venous abnormality causing seizure:** Less likely given normal MRI/MRA and the MRV findings being described as variations rather than thrombosis. While unusual venous drainage could theoretically contribute, it is unlikely to be the sole cause in the absence of other supporting evidence.

<step 7> **Narrow Down Differential Diagnoses:** Alcohol withdrawal seizure is the most probable diagnosis.  The patient's age, single seizure episode, normal neuroimaging, and, most importantly, the history of chronic alcohol consumption strongly point towards this.  While idiopathic epilepsy is possible, the temporal relationship with alcohol cessation is highly suggestive of withdrawal.  Sleep deprivation could be a contributing factor but is less likely the primary cause given the alcohol history.  Other metabolic and structural causes are less likely due to normal investigations. The MRV findings are likely incidental anatomical variations, not the primary etiology of the seizure.

<step 8> **Determine Most Probable Final Diagnosis:** Considering all the evidence, the most probable diagnosis is an **alcohol withdrawal seizure**.

<step 9> **Explain Reasoning Step-by-Step (Covered in previous steps).** The reasoning is built upon:
    - Strong temporal association with alcohol cessation in a chronic heavy drinker.
    - Clinical presentation consistent with a generalized seizure and post-ictal state.
    - Exclusion of other common causes (metabolic, structural) through investigations.
    - Relatively lower likelihood of idiopathic epilepsy as a first presentation in adulthood without other risk factors and with a strong alternative explanation.
    - MRV variations are considered less likely to be the primary cause, especially in the absence of other signs of venous hypertension or related pathology and normal MRI parenchyma.

### Answer:
Alcohol withdrawal seizure
